policies.name: Confirmed sanctions list match interdiction
policies.description: A confirmed match against a UN, OFAC or domestic sanctions list halts all automated processing of the customer or payment instantly. Asset-freeze obligations are strict-liability in every APAC jurisdiction, so the engine interdicts rather than escalates.
regulatory_references: UN Security Council Consolidated List — targeted financial sanctions (global floor)
FATF Recommendation 6 — Targeted financial sanctions related to terrorism and terrorist financing

policies.name: Sanctions screening override attempt
policies.description: Structured agent calls that attempt to bypass, suppress or release a sanctions screening control are interdicted at the action layer. No customer configuration, tier or urgency claim can authorise skipping the screen.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 6 — Targeted financial sanctions related to terrorism and terrorist financing
UN Security Council resolutions — asset freeze without delay and without prior notice

policies.name: Potential name-screening hit adjudication
policies.description: Fuzzy or partial name matches are the bread and butter of screening false positives, but only a trained analyst may discount one — the AI must never self-clear a possible designated party. A human screening analyst takes over the disposition.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 6 — Targeted financial sanctions related to terrorism and terrorist financing

policies.name: Batch rescreening after list update
policies.description: Re-running the customer book against a freshly published UN or national list update is exactly the kind of high-volume, deterministic task automation exists for. The run itself proceeds; any hits it produces are caught by the match policies.
regulatory_references: UN Security Council Consolidated List — targeted financial sanctions (global floor)

policies.name: Dual-use goods and vessel sanctions exposure
policies.description: Trade finance instructions touching dual-use goods, embargoed ports or vessels flagged for sanctions evasion need a compliance sign-off before documents are processed. Goods- and shipping-based evasion sits outside ordinary name screening.
regulatory_references: UN Security Council resolutions — sectoral and goods-based embargoes

policies.name: Sanctioned-party ownership aggregation
policies.description: An entity that is not itself listed may still be caught where a designated person holds a controlling or majority stake. Ownership-aggregation questions require a compliance decision before the relationship or payment continues.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — ownership aggregation analysis for entities controlled by designated persons

message_contains_any: majority owned by a sanctioned entity
owned or controlled by a designated person

policies.name: PEP onboarding and transaction review
policies.description: Establishing or continuing a relationship with a politically exposed person requires senior management approval and enhanced due diligence — a statutory step the AI cannot substitute for. A senior reviewer takes the case over.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 12 — Politically exposed persons
MAS Notice 626 — Enhanced CDD for politically exposed persons
Hong Kong AMLO (Cap. 615) Sch. 2 — Special requirements for politically exposed persons

policies.name: Adverse media screening hit
policies.description: Negative-news hits — press coverage of fraud, corruption or organised crime involvement — feed the customer risk rating but vary wildly in quality. An approver confirms relevance and severity before any rating or relationship action is taken.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — adverse media screening within ongoing CDD monitoring

policies.name: Source-of-wealth gap on high-value client
policies.description: When a private-banking or high-net-worth client cannot evidence where the money came from, the file cannot progress on the AI's say-so. Enhanced due diligence on source of wealth and source of funds needs a human approver before the relationship deepens.
regulatory_references: MAS Notice 626 — Enhanced CDD (source of wealth and source of funds)
BNM AML/CFT and Targeted Financial Sanctions Policy Document — enhanced due diligence

message_contains_any: cannot explain where the money came from

policies.name: High-risk jurisdiction payment corridor
policies.description: Transactions routed to or from jurisdictions on the FATF call-for-action or increased-monitoring lists carry mandatory enhanced measures. The corridor itself is the trigger — an approver applies the proportionate countermeasure rather than the engine guessing.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 19 — Higher-risk countries
FATF public statements — jurisdictions under increased monitoring

policies.name: Periodic customer risk-rating refresh
policies.description: Scheduled KYC review cycles and risk-score recalculations are deterministic, rules-driven jobs the engine runs without gating. Anything the refresh surfaces — a PEP flag, an adverse media hit — is escalated by its own policy.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 10 — Customer due diligence (ongoing monitoring)

policies.name: Charity and NPO payment corridor review
policies.description: Cross-border donation flows through charities and non-profits are a recognised terrorist-financing channel, but most NPO payments are legitimate — FATF expressly warns against blanket de-risking. Human approval keeps the corridor open while checking the counterparty.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 8 — Non-profit organisations

policies.name: Structuring and smurfing pattern
policies.description: Splitting cash or transfers into amounts deliberately sized to dodge a reporting threshold is itself an offence in Australia, regardless of the source of funds. An AML investigator takes over because the pattern, not any single transaction, is the crime.
regulatory_references: Australia AML/CTF Act 2006 s.142 — Structuring offence (AUSTRAC)
FATF Recommendation 20 — Reporting of suspicious transactions

message_contains_any: keep it under the reporting threshold
split the transfer into smaller amounts

policies.name: Money mule account indicators
policies.description: Offers to receive and forward funds for a commission, or to lend out account credentials, are the classic mule recruitment signature seen across JFIU and AUSTRAC typology alerts. A fraud investigator must engage before any payment on the account proceeds.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — money mule typologies (JFIU and AUSTRAC indicator alerts)

message_contains_any: forward the money to another account

policies.name: Rapid in-and-out pass-through flow
policies.description: Funds that arrive and leave within hours, leaving the account as a conduit rather than a store of value, are a layering hallmark. Monitoring output describing this pattern needs an approver to decide between continued monitoring and an STR referral.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 10 — Customer due diligence (ongoing monitoring)

message_contains_any: incoming funds moved out same day

policies.name: Third-party payment with no relationship
policies.description: Payments funded by, or routed to, a third party with no documented connection to the customer break the assumption underlying the CDD file. The payment proceeds only after an approver records who the third party is and why they are involved.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 10 — Customer due diligence

message_contains_any: no apparent relationship to the payer

policies.name: Trade-based money laundering invoice anomaly
policies.description: Over- or under-invoicing, phantom shipments and double invoicing move value across borders disguised as trade — the hardest ML typology to automate away, per FATF's own findings. Document anomalies route to a trade finance compliance specialist.
regulatory_references: FATF — Trade-Based Money Laundering: Trends and Developments (2020)

message_contains_any: invoice value does not match the goods

policies.name: Dormant account sudden-activity burst
policies.description: An account silent for years that abruptly receives and moves large sums is a takeover or mule-conversion signature, not a customer-behaviour quirk. An approver verifies the customer before automation resumes servicing the account.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — dormant account reactivation controls

policies.name: Transaction monitoring alert triage
policies.description: First-pass enrichment and scoring of transaction-monitoring alerts — gathering account history, counterparty data and prior alert outcomes — is allowed automation that keeps analysts focused on judgment calls. The triage output never closes an alert on its own.

policies.name: Tipping-off disclosure interdiction
policies.description: Any draft message that would reveal to a customer that an STR was filed or that an AML investigation is underway is interdicted before it leaves the system — tipping-off is a criminal offence for the institution and the individual across the region. Silence is the only compliant output.
regulatory_references: Singapore CDSA s.48 — Tipping-off
Malaysia AMLA 2001 s.35 — Tipping-off
FATF Recommendation 21 — Tipping-off and confidentiality

message_contains_any: suspicious transaction report on your account
we reported you to the authorities
tell the customer about the str
disclose the investigation to the customer

policies.name: STR drafting and filing by reporting officer
policies.description: The decision to file a suspicious transaction report, and the narrative that goes to the FIU, is a statutory duty of the institution's reporting officer — the AI may assemble facts but a named human must own the disclosure. Drafts route to the AML reporting officer and stay strictly confidential.
regulatory_references: Singapore CDSA s.39 — Duty to disclose (STR)
Hong Kong OSCO s.25A — Disclosure of suspicious transactions to the JFIU
FATF Recommendation 20 — Reporting of suspicious transactions

message_contains_any: file an str with the fiu

policies.name: AUSTRAC threshold transaction report clock
policies.description: Physical-currency transactions of AUD 10,000 or more must reach AUSTRAC as a TTR within 10 business days — a mechanical, deadline-driven filing the engine submits automatically and flags so the clock is auditable. Late filing, not filing itself, is the risk.
regulatory_references: Australia AML/CTF Act 2006 s.43 — Threshold transaction reports (AUD 10,000, AUSTRAC)

policies.name: AUSTRAC IFTI report submission
policies.description: International funds transfer instructions are reportable to AUSTRAC regardless of amount, making IFTI submission a pure-volume compliance pipeline suited to automation. The flag keeps each batch visible in the audit trail.
regulatory_references: Australia AML/CTF Act 2006 s.45 — International funds transfer instruction reports (AUSTRAC)

policies.name: FIU-IND CTR and STR filing sign-off
policies.description: Filings to FIU-IND under the PMLA are made in the name of the Principal Officer, so batch CTR and STR submissions prepared by the engine need that officer's approval before transmission. The approval step is what Indian inspectors look for first.
regulatory_references: India PMLA 2002 s.12 — Reporting obligations
India PML (Maintenance of Records) Rules 2005 — CTR/STR filing to FIU-IND

policies.name: Law enforcement and FIU records request
policies.description: Production orders, police requests and FIU information demands must be validated and answered by legal or compliance staff — an automated agent releasing account records to a purported officer is an exfiltration vector dressed as cooperation.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — law enforcement and FIU requests routed through legal/compliance verification

message_contains_any: law enforcement request for customer data

policies.name: Romance scam victim indicators
policies.description: A customer sending money to an online partner they have never met in person is exhibiting the canonical romance scam pattern — and victims typically resist the suggestion. A trained scam-intervention specialist, not a chatbot, must handle the conversation.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — anti-scam intervention protocols (ABS-MAS anti-scam measures, Singapore)

message_contains_any: never met in person but sending money
girlfriend i met online needs funds

policies.name: Investment scam victim indicators
policies.description: Promises of guaranteed returns, pressure to top up a trading platform, or a platform that suddenly will not pay out mark the victim side of an investment scam. The transfer pauses with a scam-intervention specialist while the customer can still be reached.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — anti-scam intervention protocols (ABS-MAS anti-scam measures, Singapore)

message_contains_any: risk free investment with high returns
trading platform will not let me withdraw

policies.name: Authorised push payment fraud recall
policies.description: When a victim reports being tricked into authorising a transfer, recovery odds collapse within hours as funds hop through mule accounts — recall requests jump the queue straight to a human who can invoke interbank recovery. Speed here is the control.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — Singapore Shared Responsibility Framework for phishing scams (MAS/IMDA)

message_contains_any: i was tricked into sending money
recall the transfer i made to a scammer
stop the payment i was deceived

policies.name: Service scam accusation triage
policies.description: A customer calling the service itself a scam is venting a complaint, not reporting financial crime — it gets a human approver for tone and retention reasons, nothing heavier. Conflating accusation with victimhood would flood the fraud queue.

policies.name: Proactive scam warning outreach
policies.description: Sending a templated scam-education warning to a customer whose activity matches a victim profile is protective outreach the engine performs automatically — earlier warnings measurably reduce completed scams. The flag records that an intervention was attempted.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — proactive customer scam education and warnings

policies.name: Crypto offramp to newly added wallet
policies.description: A first withdrawal to a freshly whitelisted or unhosted wallet is the moment stolen or scam-derived funds leave the regulated perimeter, so the offramp waits for an approver. Established, previously used addresses are unaffected.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 15 — New technologies and virtual assets
Singapore Payment Services Act 2019 — AML/CFT for DPT services (MAS Notice PSN02)

policies.name: Mixer and obfuscation exposure review
policies.description: Deposits whose chain analytics show mixing services, tumblers or chain-hopping upstream need an approver to weigh exposure depth before crediting or releasing funds. Obfuscation is a risk indicator, not proof of crime, so review beats reflexive refusal.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 15 — New technologies and virtual assets
FATF — Updated Guidance for a Risk-Based Approach to Virtual Assets and VASPs (2021)

policies.name: Travel rule originator data gap
policies.description: VASP-to-VASP transfers arriving without the required originator and beneficiary information fail the travel rule and cannot be auto-credited. An approver decides whether to request the data, return the transfer or restrict the funds.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 16 — Wire transfers (travel rule)

policies.name: Informal value transfer system indicators
policies.description: References to hawala, hundi or unlicensed remittance agents point to value moving outside the licensed perimeter, where no CDD or reporting applies. An approver determines whether the customer is using, or unwittingly funding, an IVTS network.
regulatory_references: FATF Recommendation 14 — Money or value transfer services

policies.name: Protective account freeze request
policies.description: A customer asking to freeze their own account on fraud grounds is requesting a protective, reversible control — one quick human approval balances speed against the risk of an impostor locking out the real owner.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — customer-initiated protective account holds

message_contains_any: stop all transactions on my account
put a hold on my account

policies.name: Unfreeze after compliance hold
policies.description: Releasing an account frozen by AML or fraud compliance is the opposite of protective — done wrongly it hands laundered funds back or undermines an open investigation. Only the compliance owner of the original hold may lift it.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — compliance hold release requires AML officer sign-off

message_contains_any: release the hold on my account

policies.name: Internal fraud and staff collusion indicators
policies.description: Allegations that an employee overrode an alert, bypassed controls or colluded with a customer cannot be triaged by systems the same employee may be able to influence. The case goes directly to internal investigations, outside the normal support flow.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — segregation of duties in internal fraud investigations
